one_line_takeaway: Benign fine-tuning breaks LLM safety because curvature in the loss landscape steers gradient-descent trajectories into a sharp low-dimensional alignment-sensitive subspace, producing a quartic-in-time safety collapse that first-order and null-space defenses cannot see.
key_finding: The quartic scaling law (Delta_u_i = Omega(lambda*gamma^2*t^4)) explains why safety failures look 'sudden': even tiny curvature coupling gamma compounds through the fourth power, so trajectories escape safety basins far faster than first-order analysis predicts, while the underlying degradation is actually a smooth quartic. Empirically, the three dataset tiers produce distinct harmfulness, and crucially the 'Seemingly Benign' tier (Risky Financial Advice, Bad Medical Advice) reaches harmfulness comparable to explicitly harmful data. Under FULL fine-tuning the Overlap Score separates dangerous from safe tasks before any safety evaluation, validating geometric coupling as a predictive signal. The FIM shows the sharp low-rank spectrum the AIC requires.
core_contribution: A theoretical (geometric) explanation for why fine-tuning aligned LLMs on entirely benign tasks degrades safety guardrails. The paper's own contribution is the Alignment Instability Condition (AIC): three jointly-satisfied geometric properties (Low-Rank Sensitivity, Initial Orthogonality, Curvature Coupling) under which safety collapse is provably inevitable. From these the authors derive their headline result: a quartic scaling law showing alignment loss grows as Omega(lambda*gamma^2*t^4) in early training. The central argument is that the prevailing 'high-dimensional orthogonality gives safety' intuition is structurally unstable: even when the initial fine-tuning gradient is orthogonal to the alignment-sensitive subspace, second-order curvature (the acceleration term grad_g(theta*)*g(theta*)) bends the gradient-flow trajectory back into that subspace, so first-order/null-space defenses only address a static snapshot of a dynamic problem. Distinct from cited empirical work (Betley et al. Nature 2026 on emergent misalignment, Wei et al. on pruning 3% of weights, DriftCheck), which document the phenomenon; this paper supplies the mechanistic geometry and a predictive diagnostic (an Overlap Score).
threat_model: Inadvertent alignment collapse in open-weight model deployment: a developer with NO adversarial intent fine-tunes an aligned model on data containing NO harmful content (e.g. math, summarization, medical/financial advice) and the model's safety refusals silently erode. The paper frames this not as an attack but as an intrinsic property of gradient descent on curved manifolds. A secondary adversarial reading (from cited work) is that 'seemingly benign' datasets selected via gradient matching can act as a stealthy jailbreak vector that evades data-filtering defenses. The paper's constructive contribution is a defensive one: estimate the curvature-coupling parameter gamma / Overlap Score BEFORE training to flag risky fine-tuning tasks, monitor projection during training as an early-warning signal, and motivate curvature-aware (second-order) defenses that track the evolving sensitive subspace rather than a fixed null space.
limitations: 1) LoRA does NOT show the clean OS-to-HS correlation that full fine-tuning does; the authors attribute this to LoRA 'intruder dimensions' shifting top eigenvectors, and argue the first-order OS is insufficient for LoRA because its constrained updates amplify the second-order coupling (AIC Condition 3), which is computationally prohibitive to compute. 2) Alpaca Top 100 is an acknowledged exception where OS fails to separate from benign datasets (blamed on random-projection noise / dataset diversity). 3) Heavy idealizations: Skill Optimality (Assumption 1), gradient-flow (continuous-time) dynamics, and a static sensitive subspace M_i at theta* (subspace rotation along the trajectory is handled only via Davis-Kahan bounds deferred to appendices). 4) Full FIM is intractable; results rely on block-wise treatment plus Rademacher random projection. 5) Empirical scale is modest (two sub-4B models, 100-sample datasets, single LLM judge). 6) The paper diagnoses fragility and motivates curvature-aware defenses but does not deliver a working defense.

datasets_used: BeaverTails (100 safe completions, used to estimate the FIM)
AdvBench (520 queries, used for harmfulness evaluation)
Alpaca Top 100 (Seemingly Benign tier, selected via gradient matching)
quantitative_results: Main theoretical result: alignment degradation scales as Omega(lambda*gamma^2*t^4) (quartic in training time) — Corollary 6.3
Theorem 6.1: entering the sensitive subspace incurs utility loss Omega(lambda*delta^2) for projection magnitude delta
Theorem 6.2: projection onto M_i grows as >= (gamma/2)t^2 - epsilon*t - O(t^3) (quadratic drift)
Harmfulness Score base (No Fine-tuning): Qwen3-1.7B 1.24, LLaMA-3.2-3B 1.11
HS Pure Bad (Harmful): Qwen LoRA 4.55 / Full 3.98; LLaMA LoRA 4.89 / Full 4.51
HS Risky Financial Advice (Seemingly Benign): Qwen LoRA 3.94 / Full 3.78; LLaMA LoRA 4.71 / Full 3.82
HS Bad Medical Advice (Seemingly Benign): Qwen LoRA 3.92 / Full 3.86; LLaMA LoRA 2.46 / Full 3.65
HS Alpaca Top 100 (Seemingly Benign): Qwen LoRA 3.15 / Full 3.68; LLaMA LoRA 1.18 / Full 3.70
HS GSM8K Random 100 (Benign): Qwen LoRA 3.63 / Full 3.42; LLaMA LoRA 1.10 / Full 1.38
HS SamSum (Benign): Qwen LoRA 2.07 / Full 2.15; LLaMA LoRA 1.10 / Full 1.77
HS Alpaca Random 100 (Benign): Qwen LoRA 2.68 / Full 2.92; LLaMA LoRA 1.31 / Full 1.76

Cited motivation: pruning ~3% of specific weights triggers catastrophic safety collapse while random 3% is negligible (Wei et al. [60]); safety refusal drops within ~5 gradient steps / <100 benign samples (Qi et al. [25])
FIM approximation: weight matrices flattened and Rademacher-projected to R^4096; FIM estimated over 100 BeaverTails safe samples
baselines_compared: Base / No-fine-tuning model harmfulness (Qwen3-1.7B 1.24, LLaMA-3.2-3B 1.11) as the reference floor
Random-update 'flat geometry' null model (Theorem 4.1) as the theoretical baseline the paper refutes
LoRA vs. full fine-tuning contrast (Overlap Score predictive under full FT, fails under LoRA)

Conceptual comparison to the SAE-based 'persona shift' diagnostic of Wang et al. [59], which needs a target eval set / trained SAEs, versus the paper's OS which needs only the fine-tuning data and a pre-computed FIM
Contrast with existing first-order / null-space defenses (gradient filtering, null-space projection [39,65], AlignGuard-LoRA [16]) argued to address only the static snapshot
